condition.qualitative_description: Surface water body total area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating both contraction of existing storage and an absolute infrastructure gap.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In arid and semi-arid alluvial plains of Rajasthan and Kachchh, surface water bodies (tanks, ponds, check dams) are the primary irrigation buffer between monsoon seasons. A declining trend in total SWB area combined with a low count of structures signals that storage infrastructure is either silting up, falling into disrepair, or was never adequately built. Dashora et al. (2019) documented that a 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat had filled completely with silt at 40 cm/year, eliminating storage capacity entirely, and that siltation reduces both storage volume and infiltration rates — directly impairing the irrigation water supply that farmers depend on for rabi and zaid seasons.

condition.qualitative_description: The mean ratio of SWB rabi area to kharif area is below 0.25, indicating that surface water bodies dry out rapidly after the monsoon and cannot sustain irrigation into the rabi season.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A low rabi-to-kharif SWB area ratio reveals that water bodies lose most of their storage within the kharif season and cannot carry water into rabi. This is a direct indicator of seasonal irrigation failure — the core stress described by farmers in arid zones. Dashora et al. (2019) showed that check dam spill accounted for 57–87% of inflow in wetter years, meaning poorly maintained or under-capacity structures lose water rapidly rather than retaining it for dry-season use. In arid alluvial plains where mean annual precipitation is below 400 mm (AER-2), this rapid drying is especially damaging to rabi crop water availability.

condition.qualitative_description: 20 or fewer cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA SWC investment means that opportunities to build or rehabilitate check dams, farm ponds, and percolation structures have not been taken up. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated a benefit-to-cost ratio of 4.1 for check dams in Rajasthan, confirming that such structures are economically attractive — their absence therefore represents a missed intervention rather than an inherent resource constraint. In the arid belt of Rajasthan and Kachchh, where natural recharge is limited to brief monsoon pulses, the absence of engineered storage amplifies seasonal water scarcity for rabi and zaid crops.

condition.qualitative_description: The nearest agricultural cooperative is more than 15 km away and no named river passes through or adjacent to the MWS, indicating both limited access to institutional credit for irrigation investment and absence of surface flow for diversion or lift irrigation.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Access to institutional credit through cooperatives (PACS) enables farmers to invest in micro-irrigation equipment, solar pumps, and tank repair. Remote MWSs with no cooperative access face higher barriers to adopting efficiency-improving technologies. The absence of a named river further eliminates the option of canal diversion or lift irrigation from perennial surface flows, leaving farmers entirely dependent on degraded local storage infrastructure. Together these structural factors compound the irrigation challenge in arid alluvial plains.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that well or borewell depth has increased noticeably over recent years, or that pumping costs have risen substantially, suggesting declining groundwater levels that increase the cost and difficulty of lift irrigation — particularly for marginal farmers.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In alluvial aquifers of arid Rajasthan and Haryana, groundwater is often the fallback when surface storage fails. However, if water tables are declining — as indicated by increasing well depth — the cost of lifting groundwater rises, disadvantaging marginal and small farmers who cannot afford deeper borewells or higher energy costs. Dashora et al. (2019) noted that check dam recharge directly raised groundwater levels in proximal wells, confirming the link between surface storage infrastructure and groundwater availability for lift irrigation. Where surface infrastructure is degraded and groundwater is deepening, both irrigation pathways are simultaneously compromised.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing water availability independent of infrastructure condition
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm against the AER baseline (<400 mm for AER-2, 400–740 mm for AER-4). If drought_severe_return_period is less than 4 years and trend_annual_precipitation_mm is strongly negative, drought is the primary driver rather than infrastructure failure. Infrastructure failure is more likely when SWB area is declining even in years with near-normal rainfall — check whether trend_swb_total_area_ha is negative in years when annual_precipitation_mm is at or above the long-term mean.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater over-extraction (not infrastructure failure) as the primary cause of irrigation water scarcity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If trend_annual_delta_g_mm is strongly negative and swb_count and swb_total_area_ha are stable or increasing, the stress pathway is groundwater depletion rather than surface irrigation infrastructure failure. The irrigation_challenges pathway is confirmed when SWB metrics are declining or low and NREGA investment is absent, pointing to structural under-investment rather than aquifer exhaustion.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers in this area noticed that they need to dig or drill deeper to find water compared to 5–10 years ago? Has the cost of pumping water for irrigation increased significantly? If possible, can you share approximate well depths (in metres) for recent years?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If well depth has increased by more than 3–5 metres over the past decade, this confirms that surface irrigation infrastructure failure is pushing farmers toward groundwater, which is itself becoming more costly and difficult to access. This strengthens the irrigation_challenges diagnosis and elevates the recommendation priority for solar pump subsidies and tank rehabilitation to reduce dependence on deepening groundwater.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Provides quantitative evidence on check dam siltation rates (40 cm/year in Gujarat), benefit-cost ratios (4.1) for check dams in Rajasthan, the effect of desilting on recharge rates (84% increase after manual desilting), and the direct link between surface storage infrastructure condition and groundwater recharge for irrigation in arid alluvial settings.

sources.title: Global Monthly Water Scarcity: Blue Water Footprints versus Blue Water Availability
sources.relevance_note: Establishes the global context of seasonal water scarcity, noting that 2.67 billion people face severe water scarcity for at least one month per year, with arid river basins in India among the most affected — supporting the seasonal irrigation failure framing for rabi and zaid seasons.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Documents increasing climate variability and water stress in South Asian arid and semi-arid regions, providing the broader context for why irrigation infrastructure resilience is critical in Rajasthan and Kachchh where rainfall is already marginal and increasingly erratic.

overall_reasoning_note: Confirm irrigation challenges when at least 2 of the primary signals — sig_01 (SWB area loss > 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 water bodies), sig_02 (Low rabi-to-kharif surface water body ratio), sig_05 (Farmer-reported well shallowing or unreliable irrigation water) — co-occur. Amplifying signals (do not alone confirm): sig_03 (20 or fewer cumulative MGNREGA SWC/irrigation works), sig_04 (No river in MWS and cooperative > 15 km away).
